reasoning: The FRED linear trend model estimates a 0.25 probability of the balance sheet exceeding its current level in the short term, which aligns well with the ongoing (but tapered) Quantitative Tightening framework. As of April 1, 2025, the Treasury runoff cap was drastically reduced to $5 billion/month. This flattening of the downward trend increases the role of natural statistical noise, slightly elevating the probability of an increase over 1-month horizons. For the 3-month and 6-month horizons, the probability curves closer to 0.50 due to major macroeconomic regime shifts on the horizon. Analysts widely project QT to fully terminate in Q2 or Q3 2025. Additionally, the reinstatement of the debt ceiling in January 2025 implies the Treasury will exhaust extraordinary measures by summer; a subsequent debt limit resolution will trigger a massive Treasury General Account (TGA) rebuild, draining bank reserves and potentially forcing the Fed to expand its balance sheet via repo operations (similar to September 2019). Thus, uncertainty naturally and significantly increases with the forecast horizon.
